STP shields must always be grounded at both ends simultaneously; single-point grounding always causes complete shielding failure and makes STP perform worse than UTP
STP shields should never be grounded; the shield floats electrically and functions as a passive reflector without requiring any grounding connection at either end

explanations.a: TIA-568 recommends two-end grounding for maximum protection; single-end grounding is a conditional compromise for ground loop prevention not the standard recommendation.
explanations.b: Both grounding approaches have legitimate use cases; the decision depends on ground potential difference between the two termination points; equi-potential bonding enables safe two-end grounding; significant ground potential differences require single-end grounding to prevent ground loops. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Single-end grounding does not cause complete shielding failure; it provides partial protection while avoiding ground loops; it is an established installation technique for specific environments.
explanations.d: STP shields must be grounded to function; an ungrounded floating shield provides no EMI protection and can actually worsen performance by acting as an antenna.

questions.text: A network engineer runs 'show interface' on a switch port and sees CRC errors incrementing at approximately 50 per minute. The connected device is a desktop computer 22 meters away with a Cat6 cable. What crosstalk-related causes should be investigated?

answers.text: CRC errors on a 22-meter Cat6 run indicate the cable is too long; shorten the cable to under 20 meters to eliminate CRC errors

CRC errors at 50 per minute on a 22-meter Cat6 run (well within specifications) suggest a local termination or installation quality issue rather than a distance problem; crosstalk-related causes to investigate: connector termination quality at the desktop outlet — inspect the jack to verify pairs were terminated with minimal untwisted length (13mm maximum); if pairs were untwisted excessively during termination the NEXT between pairs at the connector causes bit errors appearing as CRC errors; the desktop end is more prone to damage and poor termination because it is handled by users (cable pulled, stepped on, pinched by furniture); check the patch cord at the desktop — a damaged or poor quality patch cord with excessive untwist or a kink near a connector often causes NEXT-induced CRC errors; check the patch cord at the patch panel end which may have been replaced with a low-quality patch cord; check if the cable runs near any local EMI sources (UPS in the office, power strip directly adjacent); reseat all connectors and replace the patch cord first (most common fix); if errors persist inspect the wall jack with a punch-down tool check and re-terminate if the untwist exceeds spec; run a cable certification test to identify the specific failing parameter (NEXT, insertion loss, or return loss) which pinpoints the fault location
50 CRC errors per minute is within normal operating parameters for all Ethernet links; no investigation is needed unless errors exceed 1000 per minute
CRC errors on a Cat6 link always indicate a speed mismatch between the switch and the computer; configure both to 100 Mbps to eliminate CRC errors caused by gigabit timing issues

explanations.a: 22 meters is well within Cat6 specifications for all Ethernet speeds; length is not the cause at this distance.
explanations.b: Connector termination quality at the desktop outlet (excessive untwist causing NEXT), damaged patch cords, and local EMI sources are the correct crosstalk-related causes to investigate for CRC errors on a short well-within-spec Cat6 run. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Any non-zero CRC error rate warrants investigation; 50 per minute is significant and indicates a real problem causing packet loss and retransmissions.
explanations.d: Speed mismatches between gigabit devices cause late collisions and drops not CRC errors; CRC errors indicate signal corruption not timing problems from speed negotiation.
